1: YHWH rōʿî lōʾ ʾeḥsār (YHWH rōʿî lōʾ ʾeḥsār): 'The LORD is my shepherd; I shall not want.' The opening declaration YHWH rōʿî ('the LORD is my shepherd') is the psalm's entire theology in three words: divine identity (YHWH), relational role (rōʿeh/'shepherd'), and personal claim (rōʿî/'my shepherd'). The shepherd metaphor (rāʿâ) in the ANE was applied to kings — the king as shepherd of his people — but here inverted: the divine King functions as the personal shepherd of the individual. The result: lōʾ ʾeḥsār ('I shall not want/lack') — a permanent negation of deprivation. Jesus applies this psalm to himself in John 10:11, 14: 'I am the good shepherd.'
2: binʾôt dešeʾ yarbîṣēnî (ḇinʾôṯ dešeʾ yarḇîṣēnî): 'He makes me lie down in green pastures.' The hiphil yarbîṣēnî ('he makes me lie down') is causative: the lying down is not self-initiated but given by the shepherd. The phrase binʾôt dešeʾ ('in green/tender grass pastures') — the nāʾweh ('beautiful pasture') lush with vegetation — captures provision. The second image, ʿal-mê mĕnûḥôt yĕnahălēnî, 'he leads me beside still waters,' uses the hiphil nāhal ('to lead to a watering place') — a term specifically for leading animals to water. The mê mĕnûḥôt ('waters of stillness/rest') are not turbulent; they are the calm pools from which sheep can drink without fear.
3: napšî yĕšôbēb (napšî yĕšôḇēḇ): 'He restores my soul.' The polel yĕšôbēb (from šûb, 'to turn/return') means 'he causes to return, restores.' The soul (nepeš) exhausted or diverted is brought back to its proper orientation by the shepherd. The phrase yanhēnî bĕmaʿgĕlê ṣedeq, 'he leads me in paths of righteousness,' uses maʿgāl ('track, well-worn path') — the grooves worn by repeated passage through correct routes. The decisive qualifier: lĕmaʿan šĕmô ('for his name's sake') — the shepherd's guidance serves not primarily the sheep's comfort but the divine reputation. YHWH leads rightly because his name is at stake.
4: gam kî ʾēlēk bĕgê ṣalmāwet lōʾ ʾîrāʾ rāʿ (ḡam kî ʾēlēk bĕḡê ṣalmāwet lōʾ ʾîrāʾ rāʿ): 'Even though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil.' The phrase gê ṣalmāwet ('valley of deep darkness/shadow of death') — from ṣalmāwet ('deep darkness, death-shadow') — describes the most terrifying terrain. The ḡam kî ('even though, even if') is concessive: the peril is real, not imaginary. The consequent: lōʾ ʾîrāʾ rāʿ ('I will not fear evil') — complete fearlessness grounded in divine presence: kî ʾattâ ʿimmādî, 'for you are with me.' The instruments šibṭĕkā ûmišʿantekā, 'your rod and your staff,' are the shepherd's tools — the šēbeṭ for protection against predators, the mišʿenet for guiding sheep.
5: taʿărōk lĕpānay šûlḥān neged ṣōrĕrāy (taʿărōḵ lĕp̄ānay šûlḥān negeḏ ṣōrĕrāy): 'You prepare a table before me in the presence of my enemies.' The shift in imagery from shepherd/sheep to host/guest is striking — v.5 enters the realm of banquet hospitality. The table (šulḥān) set before (lĕpānay) the psalmist neged ṣōrĕrāy ('in the sight of my adversaries') is a public act: the enemies witness the psalmist being honored. The verb diššantā bĕšemen rōʾšî, 'you anoint my head with oil,' describes the honored guest receiving perfumed oil at a feast. The concluding kôsî rĕwāyâ, 'my cup overflows,' uses the niphal of rāwâ ('to be saturated, overflow') — abundance beyond capacity.
6: ʾak ṭôb wāḥesed yirdĕpûnî kol-yĕmê ḥayyāy (ʾaḵ ṭôḇ wāḥeseḏ yirḏĕp̄ûnî ḵol-yĕmê ḥayyāy): 'Surely goodness and steadfast love shall follow me all the days of my life.' The particle ʾak ('surely, only') is emphatic certainty. The verb rādap ('pursue, follow hotly') — normally used of enemies pursuing — is here applied to ṭôb wāḥesed ('goodness and steadfast love'): divine blessings as relentless pursuers who will not allow the psalmist to escape them. The final phrase wĕšabttî bĕbêt YHWH lĕʾōrek yāmîm, 'and I shall dwell in the house of the LORD forever,' uses šûb ('return, dwell') — the psalmist's permanent orientation is toward the divine dwelling. The psalm ends where theology must end: in the house of the shepherd-king.
